name: Old Watchtower
description: A crumbling watchtower rises from the village's eastern edge, its stones held together by stubbornness and ivy. A rusted telescope sits on the observation platform, pointed vaguely skyward as though it gave up on watching anything specific long ago. The railing is decorative at best, structural at worst, and trustworthy not at all.

exits.label: bridge
exits.blockedMessage: The path to the forest bridge is overgrown and impassable. You feel you haven't seen enough of the world yet to find your way through.

responses.look: A brass telescope, corroded green with age. The lens housing is cracked, and the tripod leans at an angle that suggests it has given up on perpendicularity. Without a replacement lens, it's just an expensive tube for looking at a blur.
responses.use: You peer through the telescope. Everything is a blur. The lens is cracked beyond usefulness. You'd need a replacement lens to see anything meaningful.
responses.take: The telescope is bolted to the platform. Whoever installed it was paranoid about telescope theft, which is either commendable foresight or deeply concerning.
responses.talk: You whisper encouraging words to the telescope. It does not improve. Morale is low in the optics department.
responses.default: You fiddle with the telescope. It swivels reluctantly, squeaking in protest. Without a working lens, it shows nothing but artistic blur.
responses.open: You try to pry open the lens housing. It's rusted shut, fused by decades of neglect into a single corroded unit.
responses.push: You push the telescope. It swivels grudgingly on its mount, squealing like a cat in a bath. It now points at a slightly different section of blur.
responses.pull: You pull the telescope toward you. The eyepiece lurches uncomfortably close to your face, offering an intimate view of absolutely nothing.

responses.look: The watchtower railing is more decorative suggestion than structural barrier. Several sections have rusted through entirely, offering picturesque views of a very long drop. A sign reading 'DO NOT LEAN' has itself fallen over, which rather proves its point.
responses.use: You gently test the railing. It wobbles. You step back. The railing sighs with relief.
responses.push: You push the railing. A section gives way and tumbles into the void below. The clatter echoes for an uncomfortably long time. Perhaps... don't push more railings.
responses.take: A piece of railing comes away in your hand. You put it back. Quickly. Before the rest of the railing gets ideas.
responses.default: You eye the railing with the suspicion it deserves.
responses.talk: You address the railing sternly, reminding it of its structural responsibilities. It creaks noncommittally.
responses.open: The railing is not a door. It's barely a railing. Asking it to be something else entirely seems unfair to everyone involved.
responses.pull: You pull the railing toward you. It bends inward with a groan of rusty protest, then slowly bends back, swaying like a drunk trying to look sober.

responses.look: From the watchtower, you can see the forest stretching in every direction, the village below, and -- in the far distance -- the hazy outline of something large. A castle, perhaps? Mountains? An unusually ambitious cloud? Hard to tell without a working telescope.
responses.use: You shade your eyes and look into the distance. There's definitely something out there beyond the forest, but without magnification, it's just a grey smudge on the horizon. Could be a castle. Could be weather.
responses.default: You admire the view. It's scenic, if you ignore the encroaching petrification of everything below.
responses.take: You want to take the view. It exists in the space between your eyes and the horizon. You can't fold it up and pocket it, though several artists have tried.
responses.talk: You shout into the distance. The wind carries your words away to places that don't care. An echo returns, but it sounds vaguely mocking.
responses.open: You can't open a view. It's already open. That's the entire point of views.
responses.push: You lean forward to push the... view? The view remains unmoved. Figuratively and literally.
responses.pull: You reach out as if to pull the horizon closer. It stays exactly where it is, smugly distant.

responses.look: A distant mountain range lines the horizon, its blue-grey peaks jagged against the sky like the teeth of something enormous and sleeping. The kingdom stretches all the way to their feet, which means the curse probably does too. On a clear day, you can almost convince yourself they look peaceful. Today is not that day.
responses.take: You want to take the mountains. They're several hundred miles away and weigh approximately everything. Perhaps lower your ambitions slightly.
responses.use: The mountains are decorative at this distance. Useful only for making you feel small and insignificant, which they accomplish effortlessly.
responses.default: You gaze at the distant mountains. They gaze at nothing, being mountains. You envy their composure.
responses.talk: You talk to the Mountains. It says nothing back. You choose to interpret this as deep, contemplative agreement.
responses.open: The Mountains doesn't open. You try from several angles, each equally unsuccessful. At some point, you'll accept this.
responses.push: You lean into the Mountains and push. Nothing happens, except a mild strain in your lower back. The Mountains wins this round.
responses.pull: You heave at the Mountains. Your muscles protest, the Mountains doesn't. It has the structural advantage of not caring.

responses.look: Thick ivy clings to the watchtower walls with the tenacity of a debt collector. Its roots have worked into every crack and crevice, slowly and patiently dismantling masonry that took teams of builders to erect. Nature is nothing if not persistently petty.
responses.take: You pull at the ivy. A small section comes away, revealing crumbling mortar beneath. The tower shudders almost imperceptibly. Perhaps leave the structural vegetation alone.
responses.use: You consider climbing the ivy. It looks sturdy enough, but the last person who tested that theory is presumably at the bottom of the tower. In several pieces.
responses.default: You regard the ivy covering the tower. It's winning the long war against architecture, one root at a time.
responses.talk: You speak to the Tower Ivy in a calm, measured tone. The Tower Ivy maintains its silence, which is more than can be said for most things in this kingdom.
responses.open: You try to open the Tower Ivy. It has no hinges, no latch, and no sympathy for your confusion. It is what it is.
responses.push: You shove the Tower Ivy with all your might. All your might turns out to be insufficient. The Tower Ivy remains unmoved, both physically and emotionally.
responses.pull: You tug at the Tower Ivy. It refuses to move, with the quiet determination of something that has been here much longer than you and intends to stay.

responses.look: The observation platform is a generous name for what is essentially a flat stone surface with ambitions. Flagstones are cracked and uneven, with weeds pushing through the gaps. It creaks when you shift your weight, which is not the reassurance you were hoping for from the thing between you and a fatal drop.
responses.take: You consider prying up a flagstone. But removing pieces of the floor while standing on said floor ranks among the worse ideas you've had today.
responses.use: You're standing on it. That's the primary function of a platform. You're already using it to its full potential.
responses.talk: You address the platform, thanking it for its continued structural support. A flagstone shifts slightly underfoot.
responses.open: There's no trapdoor in the platform. You checked. Twice. Solid stone all the way down.
responses.push: You stomp on the flagstones experimentally. They hold. One wobbles. You stop stomping.
responses.pull: You try to lift a flagstone. It doesn't budge. The mortar between the stones is the only thing in this tower doing its job properly.

responses.look: A weathered wooden sign that once read 'DO NOT LEAN' in stern capital letters. It has itself fallen over and now lies on the platform, face-up, delivering its warning to the sky. The irony is so thick you could lean on it.
responses.take: You pick up the sign, consider it briefly, then put it back down. Carrying around a 'DO NOT LEAN' sign feels like a cry for help you're not ready to make.
responses.use: You prop the sign back up against the railing. It immediately falls over again. The sign has made its philosophical position on standing upright quite clear.
responses.talk: You read the sign aloud: 'DO NOT LEAN.' Sound advice for the railing, the tower, the kingdom, and frankly, your life choices.
responses.open: It's a sign, not a book. Although given the state of literacy in this kingdom, the distinction may be academic.
responses.push: You nudge the fallen sign with your foot. It slides across the flagstones. It now reads 'DO NOT LEAN' to a slightly different audience of no one.
responses.pull: You pull the sign toward you. The wood is soft with rot and a corner breaks off in your hand. The sign now reads 'DO NOT LEA.' Still good advice.

responses.look: The village sprawls below the watchtower like a collection of boxes a giant child abandoned mid-tantrum. Thatched roofs, cobbled streets, and the occasional plume of chimney smoke suggest life continues down there, oblivious to the curse slowly turning everything grey.
responses.take: You can't take an entire village. You can barely take responsibility for your own actions. One thing at a time.
responses.use: The village is below you. To use it, you'd need to go down there. This isn't a complicated logistics problem.
responses.talk: You shout down to the village. A dog barks in response. A door slams. The village has heard enough from people shouting from towers.
responses.open: You can't open a village. It's already there. It's been there for centuries.
responses.push: You can't push a village from up here. Gravity works in one direction, and the village didn't do anything to deserve being pushed.
responses.pull: You can't pull the village closer. It's exactly as far away as the bottom of the tower.

responses.look: A cracked but functional telescope lens, sitting on a dusty shelf. Someone removed it from the telescope -- or it fell out, given the general state of maintenance here. It magnifies your view and your insecurities.
responses.use: A lens by itself is just a paperweight for people with delusions of astronomy. You'd need a telescope to use it properly.
responses.take: The Telescope Lens isn't going anywhere with you. It belongs here. You, on the other hand, are debatable.
responses.talk: You talk to the Telescope Lens. It says nothing back. You choose to interpret this as deep, contemplative agreement.
responses.open: The Telescope Lens doesn't open. You try from several angles, each equally unsuccessful. At some point, you'll accept this.
responses.push: You lean into the Telescope Lens and push. Nothing happens, except a mild strain in your lower back. The Telescope Lens wins this round.
responses.pull: You heave at the Telescope Lens. Your muscles protest, the Telescope Lens doesn't. It has the structural advantage of not caring.

actions.text: You pocket the telescope lens. It's cracked but still functional -- like most things in this kingdom, including the governance. It magnifies what you look through it at, which is either useful or terrifying depending on what you point it at.

You slot the lens into the telescope housing. It fits, barely. Peering through, the blur resolves into sharp clarity. Beyond the forest, past rolling hills of increasingly grey countryside, stands Castle Erelhain -- a massive stone fortress with towers reaching skyward. Even at this distance, you can see the grey creeping up its walls. The petrification curse is spreading. Whatever is happening to this kingdom, the answer is in that castle. You should probably go there. Against your better judgment, which has been notably absent thus far.

You fit your lens into the telescope and peer through. The blur resolves into sharp clarity. Beyond the forest stands Castle Erelhain -- a massive stone fortress with the grey curse visibly creeping up its walls. The petrification is spreading faster than you'd like. Whatever is happening to this kingdom, the answer is in that castle.

fall-death.title: Death by Ambition
fall-death.narratorText: You climb the railing. The railing, as previously noted, is decorative. It was never meant to support the weight of a person, let alone a person with pockets full of quest items. The railing gives way with the resigned creak of something that warned you repeatedly. The fall is long enough for you to reflect on your choices, but not long enough to reach any useful conclusions. The ground is unimpressed by your arrival.

dynamicDescriptions.seen_castle: The watchtower's telescope now shows a clear view of Castle Erelhain in the distance. The image of the grey curse spreading up its walls lingers in your mind. You know where you need to go. You just wish you didn't.

tiers: A telescope without a lens is just an expensive tube. I wonder if there's a replacement lens lying around this watchtower somewhere. On a shelf, perhaps.
There's a cracked telescope lens on a dusty shelf in this very room. Pick it up and fit it into the telescope. Optics isn't that complicated.
Take the telescope lens from the shelf and use it on the rusted telescope. You'll see the castle in the distance. This is how telescopes work.
